title: www.ukmesh.com, Yet another MeshCore packet analzyer

messages.author: Bye Andy
messages.text: I know what your probably thinking, why do we need another packet analyzer. Well I started it as a local project for Teesside in the UK, but as we started receiving packets from the wider mesh again it started to really expand so I made the project UK wide, you can check it out on https://www.ukmesh.com , the live map is what has had the most work done to it. This is purely data that has arrived to my node in Teesside , so the data is very heavily skewed so shortly im going to be opening it up to some select areas around the UK to see how the site copes. (edit: Onboarding went incredibly well, I can take on more nodes!) The part of my project that I think really improves on prior maps is the links it can draw between nodes, it seems quite accurate so far! One of the things on my list is to improve the coverage map filter massively, I am aware how inaccurate the method is that I am using currently. its also open source on github at https://github.com/gadgethd/teesside-mesh im unsure on how well it will build on different machines but that is something we can get to when the time comes. *Also yes this project is quite similar to meshrank.net I am aware, I only found that site after starting mine up Anyway its very late here in the UK when im posting this so ill be going to bed (edited)
messages.content_html: I know what your probably thinking, why do we need another packet analyzer. Well I started it as a local project for Teesside in the UK, but as we started receiving packets from the wider mesh again it started to really expand so I made the project UK wide, you can check it out on https://www.ukmesh.com, the live map is what has had the most work done to it. This is purely data that has arrived to my node in Teesside , so the data is very heavily skewed so shortly im going to be opening it up to some select areas around the UK to see how the site copes. (edit: Onboarding went incredibly well, I can take on more nodes!) The part of my project that I think really improves on prior maps is the links it can draw between nodes, it seems quite accurate so far! One of the things on my list is to improve the coverage map filter massively, I am aware how inaccurate the method is that I am using currently. its also open source on github at https://github.com/gadgethd/teesside-mesh im unsure on how well it will build on different machines but that is something we can get to when the time comes. *Also yes this project is quite similar to meshrank.net I am aware, I only found that site after starting mine up Anyway its very late here in the UK when im posting this so ill be going to bed (edited)

messages.author: NE35

messages.content_html: It's already amazing what you have done in a short amount of time and one Mqtt node.

messages.author: Bye Andy
messages.text: Updates for tonight, ive improved to the links algorithm by including repeated hex nearby nodes to lower the confidence in that specific link, seems to have had a small effect. also now when you click on a repeater you can view the location of the nodes that share the same first 2 hex characters of the public key. there is also another layer on the map showing repeaters within a 0-3 hop range with the same starting hex using the predicted signal strength. this is the 'Hex Clashes' toggle on the map and shows the link between the two (edited)

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: A repeater-observer is likley to be placed high up with a good antenna, so it can potentially hear everything. Repeaters are busy single duplex devices, they cannot hear when they transmit and they transmit a lot. They may be in a worse position for WiFi/USB connections to send MQTT. There was some discussion of them not reporting to mqtt thier own packets, but this surely just affects their own stats. A compnanion-observer is likley to rely on a nearby repeater and not be as well placed to hear weak packets, but is more convenient to connect. It can potentially hear as much - everything the repeater repeats, more - somethings missed or not reported by a busy repeater, or less - things the repeater chose not to repeat. For a repeater to miss a packet that something else just managed to hear might not happen. If a weak packet came in during or immediatley after a repeat, any companion would be overwhelemed by the signal from the repeater and may need time for the AGC to increase. If it came in before the repeater would probably hear it and stop or corrupt it before it could complete. I think a dedicated or companion-observer would be best if it was well placed such as in a loft. Reception is easier than transmission, it doesn't need the same antenna. There is no question of it being busy and missing something or a repeater not sending everything, it potentially gets two chances. You now detach the firmware and update cycle of the repeater from any restrictions or flaws imposed by an observer. (edited)
messages.content_html: A repeater-observer is likley to be placed high up with a good antenna, so it can potentially hear everything. Repeaters are busy single duplex devices, they cannot hear when they transmit and they transmit a lot. They may be in a worse position for WiFi/USB connections to send MQTT. There was some discussion of them not reporting to mqtt thier own packets, but this surely just affects their own stats. A compnanion-observer is likley to rely on a nearby repeater and not be as well placed to hear weak packets, but is more convenient to connect. It can potentially hear as much - everything the repeater repeats, more - somethings missed or not reported by a busy repeater, or less - things the repeater chose not to repeat. For a repeater to miss a packet that something else just managed to hear might not happen. If a weak packet came in during or immediatley after a repeat, any companion would be overwhelemed by the signal from the repeater and may need time for the AGC to increase. If it came in before the repeater would probably hear it and stop or corrupt it before it could complete. I think a dedicated or companion-observer would be best if it was well placed such as in a loft. Reception is easier than transmission, it doesn't need the same antenna. There is no question of it being busy and missing something or a repeater not sending everything, it potentially gets two chances. You now detach the firmware and update cycle of the repeater from any restrictions or flaws imposed by an observer. (edited)

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: As much as I love a dark theme, I think it is very limiting here. All it can effectively show is how steep a slope is due to the vertical scale exaggeration in one direction. Oddly it's not to the south though, reversing the colours is making illumination look wrong from that direction. I think it would be better with some height and other information about the ground included. (edited)

Oddly it's not to the south though, reversing the colours is making illumination look wrong from that direction. I think it would be better with some height and other information about the ground included. (edited)

messages.content_html: I have now been sent down a rabbit hole of why maps are shaded from the north.

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: Only to be told by AI that they are not but it gives me the same answer if I say north or south. It may make it look even worse if you change it round. Perceptual relief inversion (or terrain reversal) is an optical illusion where concave landforms (valleys) appear convex (ridges) or vice-versa. Caused by the brain’s "light-from-above" assumption, this happens when shading/shadows in images (especially maps/satellite imagery) come from the wrong direction (e.g., South). Taylor & Francis Online (edited)
messages.content_html: Only to be told by AI that they are not but it gives me the same answer if I say north or south. It may make it look even worse if you change it round. Perceptual relief inversion (or terrain reversal) is an optical illusion where concave landforms (valleys) appear convex (ridges) or vice-versa. Caused by the brain’s "light-from-above" assumption, this happens when shading/shadows in images (especially maps/satellite imagery) come from the wrong direction (e.g., South). messages.author: Bye Andy
messages.text: Added a few fun UKMesh site updates recently: you can preview planned repeater coverage on the map, switch the map between dark/light mode, check the new companion activity leaderboard, share Health Check results more cleanly, see international contact activity, and get a better view of which nodes have heard a repeater. Kinda forgot to update it here. quite a few changes in the backend as well to make things more efficient

Kinda forgot to update it here. quite a few changes in the backend as well to make things more efficient

messages.author: Bye Andy
messages.text: if anyone has an existing MQTT repeater it would be great to be able to fill in more of this map geographically for the healthcheck function I added on the site, send me a DM and ill send login information for my MQTT ingest! check out the healthcheck at https://healthcheck.ukmesh.com/app

UKMesh Health Check
Generate a test code, send it to the configured channel, and watch observer coverage build in real time.
